2.1: In those days there appeared Mattathias, son of John, son of Simeon, priest of the sons of Joarib of Jerusalem, who lived in Modin.
2.2: He had five sons: John, nicknamed Gaddis,
2.5: Eleazar, nicknamed Abaron, and Jonathan, nicknamed Apphus.
2.6: Seeing the outrages that were being committed in Judah and Jerusalem,
2.7: Mathathias said, «Alas, why was I born to see the ruin of my people and the ruin of the holy city, and to remain here idle while it is delivered into the hands of the enemy?”
2.8: And that its sanctuary is in the power of foreigners? Its temple has become like the dwelling place of a vile man,
2.9: The precious objects that were its glory were carried off like spoils, its grandchildren were massacred in its streets, the enemy's sword felled its young men.
2.10: What people did not inherit their kingdom and receive their share of its spoils?
2.11: They took away all her finery; from free, she became a slave.
2.12: Everything we had that was holy, beautiful, and glorious has been ravaged and desecrated by the nations.
2.13: Why should we still be alive?»
2.14: Then Mattathias and his sons tore their clothes, covered themselves with sackcloth, and mourned deeply.
2.15: The king's officers tasked with forcing apostasy came to Modin to organize sacrifices.
2.16: A large number of Israelites joined them, and Mattathias and his sons also gathered on their side.
2.17: Antiochus' envoys, addressing Mathathias, said to him: "You are the first in this city, the greatest in regard and influence, and surrounded by sons and brothers.
2.18: "Go first and carry out the king's command, as all the nations have done, the men of Judah and those who remained in Jerusalem, and you and your family will be among the king's friends, you and your sons, and you will receive ornaments of gold and silver and many gifts."»
2.19: Mathathias replied and said aloud: «Even if all the nations that are part of Antiochus’ kingdom were to obey him, each abandoning the worship of their fathers and willingly submitting to his commands,
2.20: I, my sons and my brothers, will follow the covenant of our fathers.
2.21: May God keep us from abandoning the law and its precepts.
2.22: We will not obey the king's orders to deviate from our faith, whether to the right or to the left.»
2.23: As soon as he had finished this speech, a Jew stepped forward in full view of everyone to sacrifice, according to the king's order, on the altar erected at Modin.
2.24: At this sight, Mathathias was indignant and shuddered to the depths of his being; he let his anger rise according to the law and, rushing forward, he killed this man on the altar.
2.25: He simultaneously killed the king's officer who was forcing a sacrifice and overturned the altar.
2.26: Thus he was filled with zeal for the law, like Phinehas, who killed Zambri, son of Salum.
2.27: Then Mattathias went through the city, shouting aloud, «Whoever is zealous for the law and upholds the covenant, let him leave the city and follow me.»
2.28: And he and his sons fled to the mountains, abandoning everything they owned in the city.
2.29: A large number of Jews, seeking justice and the law, then went down into the desert,
2.30: to remain there, they, their children and their wives, as well as their livestock, because the evils that afflicted them had reached their peak.
2.31: The king's officers and troops in Jerusalem, in the city of David, were informed that some men who had disobeyed the king's order had gone down into the desert to hidden retreats.
2.32: Immediately a large number of soldiers set out in pursuit of them. When they caught up with them, they camped opposite them and prepared to attack them on the Sabbath day.
2.33: They told them, "It's enough that you've resisted until now. Go out and carry out the king's order, and you will live."«
2.34: The Jews replied, "We will not go out and we will not obey the king's order; that would be violating the Sabbath day."«
2.35: The Syrians immediately engaged them in combat.
2.36: They did not answer them, did not throw a single stone at them, and did not block their retreat.
2.37: «Let us all die,» they said, “in the simplicity of our hearts. Heaven and earth bear witness for us that you are making us die unjustly.”
2.38: The soldiers attacked them on the Sabbath day, and they, their wives and children, as well as their livestock, died; there were about a thousand men.
2.39: Mathathias and his friends learned of this massacre and they felt great sorrow.
2.40: And they said to one another, "If we all do as our brothers have done and do not fight against the nations for our lives and for our institutions, they will soon have exterminated us from the earth."«
2.41: So they made this resolution that day: «Whoever comes to war against us on the Sabbath day, let us fight him and not allow ourselves to be killed as our brothers did in their retreat.»
2.42: Then a band of Assidenes joined them, made up of valiant men of Israel, from all those whose hearts were attached to the law.
2.43: All those who were seeking to escape the present evils also came to them and increased their strength.
2.44: Having thus formed an army, they first struck the wrongdoers in their anger and the wicked in their indignation, the rest sought salvation by fleeing to the nations.
2.45: Mattathias and his sons traveled throughout the land; they destroyed the altars,
2.46: They forcibly circumcised all the uncircumcised children they found in the land of Israel,
2.47: and pursued those puffed up with pride. The enterprise succeeded under their leadership,
2.48: They upheld the cause of the law against the power of the pagans and against the power of kings, and they did not bow down before the sinner.
2.49: When the days of Mathathias came to an end, he said to his sons: «Now pride reigns and punishment prevails; it is a time of ruin and fierce anger.
2.50: Therefore, O my sons, be zealous for the law and give your lives for the covenant of our fathers.
2.51: Remember the works that our fathers accomplished in their time and you will receive glory and an immortal name.
2.52: Was not Abraham found faithful in the test, and was not his faith credited to him as righteousness?
2.53: Joseph, in the time of his affliction, kept the commandments and he became lord of Egypt.
2.54: Phinehas, our father, because he burned with zeal for the cause of God, received the assurance of a holy priesthood.
2.55: Jesus, having fulfilled the word, became a judge in Israel.
2.56: Caleb, for having borne witness in the assembly, received a portion of the land.
2.57: David, through his piety, obtained a royal throne for all ages.
2.58: Elijah, because he was very zealous for the law, was taken up to heaven.
2.59: Ananias, Azarias and Misael, having trusted, were saved from the flames.
2.60: Daniel, through his innocence, was delivered from the mouths of the lions.
2.61: Therefore, consider that in all ages, all who hope in Him do not perish.
2.62: Do not fear the threats of a sinful man, for his glory goes to corruption and worms.
2.63: He rises today and tomorrow he will no longer be found, because he will have returned to his dust and his thoughts will have vanished.
2.64: Therefore, my sons, be strong and valiant in defending the law, for by it you will be glorified.
2.65: This is Simon, your brother; I know that he is a man of counsel, always listen to him, he will be a father to you.
2.66: Let Judas Maccabeus, a valiant hero since his youth, be the leader of your army and direct the war against the peoples.
2.67: You will enlist all the observers of the law and you will avenge your people.
2.68: "Render unto the nations what they have done unto Israel, and observe the commandments of the law."»
2.69: And after he had blessed them, he was gathered to his fathers.
2.70: He died in the year one hundred and forty-six, his sons buried him in the tomb of their fathers at Modin and Israel mourned him in great grief.

3.1: Judas, his son, nicknamed Maccabeus, rose up after him.
3.2: He had as helpers all his brothers and all those who had joined his father, and together they joyfully fought the battles of Israel.
3.3: He spread the glory of his people far and wide, he donned the armor like a hero, he girded on his weapons of war and engaged in battles, protecting the camp of Israel with his sword.
3.4: In action, he was like a lion, like a lion cub roaring at its prey.
3.5: He pursued the wicked, searching their hideouts and delivered to the flames those who troubled his people.
3.6: The wicked retreated in fear before him, all the workers of iniquity were in terror, and his hand happily led the deliverance of his people.
3.7: Through his exploits he caused bitterness to several kings and joy to Jacob, and his memory is forever blessed.
3.8: He went through the cities of Judah and exterminated the wicked, and he turned away Israel's wrath.
3.9: His name became famous to the ends of the earth, and he gathered those who were about to perish.
3.10: Apollonius assembled pagan troops, a large army drawn from Samaria, to fight Israel.
3.11: As soon as Judas learned of this, he marched against him, defeated him, and killed him. A great number of enemies perished, and the rest fled.
3.12: The Jews seized their loot and Judas took Apollonius' sword and always used it in battles from then on.
3.13: Seron, commander of the Syrian army, having learned that Judas had gathered a large group of loyal Jews marching with him into battle,
3.14: He said, "I will make a name for myself and gain glory in the kingdom; I will fight Judas and those with him who despise the king's orders."«
3.15: So he made a second expedition, and with him assembled a powerful army of impious men, to help him and take revenge on the children of Israel.
3.16: When they were close to the ascent of Béthoron, Judas marched to meet them with a small troop.
3.17: When his men saw the army advancing against them, they said to Judas, "How can we, so few in number, fight against such a powerful multitude, especially since we are exhausted from fasting today?"«
3.18: Judas replied: "It is easy for a multitude to be locked in the hands of a few, for the God of heaven there is no difference in saving by a large number or by a small number.
3.19: For victory in war does not lie in the multitude of combatants, but strength comes from heaven.
3.20: They are coming against us, filled with pride and impiety, to destroy us, our wives and our children, and to plunder us.
3.21: But we are fighting for our lives and for our law.
3.22: God will crush them before us, so do not be afraid of them.»
3.23: As soon as he had finished speaking, he suddenly threw himself upon them: Seron was defeated and saw his army crushed before his eyes.
3.24: Judas pursued him down the descent from Bethoron to the plain, eight hundred men of their troops were killed and the rest fled to the land of the Philistines.
3.25: Then began to spread the fear of Judas and his brothers, and terror among the surrounding nations.
3.26: His name reached the king, and all the peoples spoke of Judas's battles.
3.27: When King Antiochus learned of this news, he was overcome with anger, he gave orders and gathered all the troops of his kingdom, a very powerful army.
3.28: He opened his treasury and gave his troops a year's pay and commanded them to be ready for anything.
3.29: Then he realized that money was lacking in his coffers and the taxes from the province brought in little, because of the troubles and evils he had unleashed in the country by wanting to abolish the laws that had been in use since ancient times.
3.30: He feared that, as had happened several times, he would not have the means to provide for the expenses and liberality that he had previously lavished in abundance and more widely than all the kings who had preceded him.
3.31: In this extreme predicament, he resolved to go to Persia to collect tribute from those provinces and raise a great deal of money.
3.32: He therefore left Lysias, a person of considerable importance and of the royal family, in charge of the affairs of the kingdom, from the Euphrates River to the borders of Egypt.,
3.33: and to take care of his son Antiochus until his return.
3.34: He entrusted him with half of his troops and the elephants and gave him orders for the execution of all his designs and especially concerning all the inhabitants of Judea and Jerusalem.
3.35: Lysias was to send an army against them to break and annihilate the power of Israel and the remnant of Jerusalem, and to erase their memory from that place.,
3.36: and to establish throughout their country sons of foreigners, to whom he would distribute their lands by lot.
3.37: Then, having taken with him the other half of his troops, the king left Antioch, his capital, in the year one hundred and forty-seven, crossed the Euphrates river and traversed the high country.
3.38: Lysias chose Ptolemy, son of Dorymenes, Nicanor and Gorgias, skilled captains and friends of the king,
3.39: and he sent with them forty thousand foot soldiers and seven thousand horsemen, to invade the land of Judah and destroy it according to the king's order.
3.40: They set out with all their troops and, having entered Judea, they camped near Emmaus, in the plain.
3.41: When the merchants of the land heard of their arrival, they took with them much silver and gold, as well as fetters, and went to the Syrian camp to buy the Israelites as slaves. The troops from Syria and the land of the Philistines joined this army.
3.42: Judas and his brothers, seeing that the situation had worsened and that the enemy armies were encamped at their borders, and having also learned of the king's order to destroy and exterminate their people,
3.43: They said to one another, "Let us rebuild the ruins of our people and fight for our people and our sanctuary."«
3.44: The assembly therefore gathers to be ready for battle and to pray and implore pity and mercy.
3.45: But Jerusalem was without inhabitants, like a desert; none of her children entered it or left it. The sanctuary was trampled underfoot, and foreigners occupied the stronghold; it was a dwelling place for the nations. Jacob's joy was gone; the flute and harp were silent.
3.46: Having gathered together, they came to Maspha, opposite Jerusalem, because there was formerly in Maspha a place of prayer for Israel.
3.47: They fasted that day, covered themselves with sackcloth, threw ashes on their heads, and tore their clothes.
3.48: They spread out the book of the law, which the nations were searching for in order to paint images of their idols on it.
3.49: They brought the priestly garments, the firstfruits and the tithes, and summoned some Nazarenes who had completed the time of their vow,
3.50: And they cried out with a loud voice to heaven, saying, «What shall we do for these men, and where shall we lead them?”
3.51: Your sanctuary has been trampled and desecrated, and your priests are in mourning and humiliation.
3.52: And now the nations have gathered against us to destroy us. You know their plans against us.
3.53: How can we stand up to them if you don't support us?»
3.54: And they sounded the trumpets and gave a loud shout.
3.55: Then Judas appointed leaders of the people: leaders of a thousand men, of a hundred, of fifty, and of ten.
3.56: And he told those who had just built a house to take wives and plant a vineyard, and those who were afraid to return each to his home, according to the law.
3.57: Then the army set off and went to camp south of Emmaus.
3.58: There Judas said to them, «Gird yourselves and be brave and be ready for tomorrow morning to fight against these nations gathered to destroy us and our sanctuary.
3.59: For it is better for us to die with weapons in hand than to see the suffering of our people and our sanctuary desecrated.
3.60: Whatever heaven wills, let it be done.»

4.1: Gorgias took with him five thousand men and a thousand elite cavalrymen, and they set out during the night.,
4.2: To approach the Jewish camp and strike them unexpectedly, the men from the fortress of Zion served as their guides.
4.3: Judas, having learned of this, rose up, he and the valiant men, to attack the king's army which was at Emmaus,
4.4: while the troops were still dispersed outside the camp.
4.5: Gorgias arrived at Judah's camp during the night, but he found no one there, so he went looking for them in the mountains, saying, "They are fleeing before us."«
4.6: As soon as day broke, Judas appeared on the plain with three thousand men, but they did not have the weapons they would have wanted, either for cover or for striking.
4.7: Upon seeing the fortified camp of the nations, the soldiers covered in breastplates, and the horsemen patrolling around them, all trained for combat,
4.8: Judas said to the men who were with him: «Do not fear their multitude and do not dread their attack.
4.9: Remember how our fathers were saved in the Red Sea, when Pharaoh was pursuing them with a powerful army.
4.10: Let us now cry out to heaven, hoping that he will deign to have mercy on us, remember his covenant with our fathers, and destroy this army before our very eyes today.
4.11: And all nations will know that there is someone who delivers and saves Israel.»
4.12: Then the strangers looked up and saw them marching against them,
4.13: And they went out of the camp to give battle, at the same time those who were with Judas sounded the trumpet.
4.14: They came to blows and the nations were defeated and fled into the plain.
4.15: The last ranks all fell by the sword and the Jews pursued them as far as Gazara and into the plains of Judea, Azotus and Jamnia and they killed nearly three thousand of them.
4.16: Then Judas, with his army, retraced his steps and ceased pursuing them.,
4.17: saying to the people: «Do not be greedy for plunder, for a battle awaits us.
4.18: Gorgias and his troops are near us in the mountains, but hold firm now against our enemies, defeat them, and then you can take their plunder without fear.»
4.19: Judas was still speaking when a division of Gorgias appeared coming out of the mountain.
4.20: They saw that their people were fleeing and that the Jews had set fire to the camp, for the smoke that could be seen showed what had happened.
4.21: At this sight, they were greatly afraid, and as they also saw Judah's army lined up on the plain, ready to give battle,
4.22: They all fled to the land of the Philistines.
4.23: Judas returned to plunder the camp; they carried off much gold and silver, as well as purple and scarlet fabrics and great riches.
4.24: Upon their return, they sang hymns, offering up praises to the Lord: "For he is good, for his mercy endures forever."«
4.25: A great deliverance was given to Israel on that day.
4.26: Those foreigners who had escaped came to tell Lysias everything that had happened.
4.27: Upon learning this news, he was saddened and dejected, because his plans against Israel had failed and the king's orders were not being carried out.
4.28: The following year, Lysias assembled an army of sixty thousand elite infantry and five thousand cavalry, in order to subdue the Jews.
4.29: They advanced towards Judea and set up camp near Beth-horon. Judas marched against them at the head of ten thousand men.
4.30: At the sight of this formidable army, he prayed, saying: «Blessed are you, O deliverer of Israel, who have broken the strength of the giant by the hand of your servant David and delivered the camp of the Philistines into the hands of Jonathan, son of Saul, and his armor-bearer.
4.31: Enclose this army in the hands of your people Israel, and let them be confused with their infantry and cavalry.
4.32: Inspire terror in them, crush their presumptuous audacity, and let them be shaken by their defeat.
4.33: "May they fall by the sword of those who love you, and may all who know your name offer you hymns of praise."»
4.34: They engaged in battle and five thousand men of Lysias' army fell before the Jews.
4.35: Seeing the rout of his army and the intrepidity of Judah's soldiers, who showed themselves willing to live or die honorably, Lysias returned to Antioch and recruited foreigners, promising himself, after increasing his army, to return to Judea.
4.36: Then Judas and his brothers said, "Now that our enemies are defeated, let us go up and purify the temple and reconsecrate it."«
4.37: The whole army gathered and they went up Mount Zion.
4.38: Seeing the sanctuary deserted, the altar desecrated, the gates burned, shrubs growing in the courtyard as in a forest or on the mountains, and the chambers destroyed,
4.39: They tore their clothes, mourned bitterly, and poured ashes on their heads,
4.40: They prostrated themselves with their faces to the ground and, while the trumpets sounded in a fanfare, shouted towards the sky.
4.41: Then Judas detached a corps of troops to fight the Syrians who were in the citadel, until the holy places were purified.
4.42: Then he chose priests without blemish, devoted to the law of God,
4.43: And they purified the sanctuary and removed the defiled stones to an unclean place.
4.44: They deliberated on what should be done to the altar of burnt offerings which had been desecrated,
4.45: And the happy thought came to them to destroy it, lest it become a disgrace to them after the pagans had defiled it. They demolished the altar,
4.46: and they placed the stones on the temple mountain, in a suitable place, awaiting the coming of a prophet who would give a decision concerning them.
4.47: And they took uncut stones, according to the law, and built a new altar on the pattern of the old one.
4.48: They rebuilt the sanctuary, as well as the interior of the temple, and they consecrated the courtyards.
4.49: They made new sacred utensils, and put the candelabra, the altar of incense, and the table back in the temple.
4.50: They burned incense on the altar, lit the lamps of the menorah, and they gave light in the temple.
4.51: They placed loaves of bread on the table and hung the sails. After they had finished all the work they had done,
4.52: They got up very early on the twenty-fifth day of the ninth month, the month named Casleu, in the year one hundred and forty-eight.,
4.53: and they offered a sacrifice, according to the law, on the new altar of burnt offerings which they had built.
4.54: In the same month and on the same day that it had been profaned by the nations, the altar was consecrated again, with the singing of psalms, and the sound of harps, lyres and cymbals.
4.55: All the people fell on his face and worshipped him, and raising their eyes to heaven, they blessed the One who had given him prosperity.
4.56: They celebrated the dedication of the altar for eight days and offered burnt offerings with joy and sacrifices of thanksgiving and praise.
4.57: They adorned the temple facade with crowns and shields and repaired the temple entrances and chambers and fitted them with doors.
4.58: There was great joy among the people, and the reproach inflicted by the nations was removed.
4.59: Judas, in agreement with his brothers and the whole assembly of Israel, established that the days of the dedication of the altar would be celebrated in their time each year for eight days, starting from the twenty-fifth of Casleu, with joy and gladness.
4.60: At that time they built on Mount Zion a high wall and strong towers, so that the nations would no longer come and trample the holy places underfoot, as they had done before.
4.61: And Judah stationed a detachment there for its guard and defense. Beth-sur was fortified so that the people would have a fortress opposite Idumea.

5.1: When the surrounding nations learned that the altar had been rebuilt and the sanctuary restored to its former state, they were very angry.
5.2: They resolved to exterminate the descendants of Jacob who lived among them, and they began to massacre many of them and pursue them.
5.3: Judas waged war against the sons of Esau in Idumea, in the land of Acrabathane, because they attacked the children of Israel; he inflicted a great defeat upon them, humiliated them, and took their plunder.
5.4: He also remembered the wickedness of the sons of Bean, who were a snare and a danger to the people, because of the traps they set for them in the roads.
5.5: He trapped them in their towers, besieged them, condemned them, and burned their towers with all those inside.
5.6: Then he went to the Ammonites and found there a strong army and a large people, whose leader was Timothy.
5.7: He fought them in many battles and they were crushed before him and he cut them to pieces.
5.8: He took Jazer and its dependencies and returned to Judea.
5.9: The nations that are in Gilead gathered together against the Israelites who lived in their territory, in order to exterminate them, and the latter took refuge in the fortress of Datheman.
5.10: They sent letters to Judas and his brothers, saying, «The nations around us have gathered against us to destroy us.
5.11: They are preparing to come and seize the fortress in which we have taken refuge, and Timothy is the leader of their army.
5.12: Come now and deliver us from their hands, for already a great number of our people have fallen.
5.13: All our brothers who were in the land of Tob were put to death; our enemies took their wives and children captive and seized their possessions; they killed nearly a thousand men there.»
5.14: We were still reading their letters when other messengers arrived from Galilee, their clothes torn, bringing this news:
5.15: «"The people of Ptolemais, Tyre, Sidon, and all the foreigners of Galilee have gathered together to destroy us."»
5.16: When Judas and the people had heard these speeches, a great assembly was held to consider what they should do for their brothers who were in tribulation and attacked by these enemies.
5.17: Judas said to his brother Simon, "Choose some men and go to rescue your brothers who are in Galilee; my brother Jonathan and I will go to Gilead."«
5.18: He left in Judea Joseph, son of Zechariah, and Azariah, leaders of the people, with the rest of the army to stand guard.
5.19: And he gave them this order: «Govern this people, but do not engage in war with the nations until our return.»
5.20: Simon was assigned three thousand men to go to Galilee and Judas eight thousand to go to Gilead.
5.21: Simon went to Galilee and fought many battles against the nations, and they were crushed before him. He pursued them to the gate
5.22: of Ptolemais. Nearly three thousand men perished from among the nations, and he carried off their plunder.
5.23: He gathered the Jews who were in Galilee and Arbates, with their wives, their children, and all that belonged to them, and brought them to Judea with great joy.
5.24: Meanwhile, Judas Maccabeus and Jonathan, his brother, crossed the Jordan and advanced three days' walk into the desert.
5.25: They met the Nabataeans, who received them with friendship and told them everything that had happened to their brothers in Gilead:
5.26: «A great number of them,” they told them, “are being held captive in Bossora and Bozor, in Alimes, Casphor, Maced and Carnaim, cities which are all fortified and large,
5.27: There are also some imprisoned in the other cities of Gilead. And their enemies are preparing to attack these fortresses tomorrow, to seize them and destroy them all in a single day.»
5.28: Judas, changing direction, took with his army a route towards the interior of the desert and suddenly appeared before Bosor, he seized the city, put the entire male population to the sword, took all their plunder and delivered the city to the flames.
5.29: He left there during the night and walked to the fortress of Datheman.
5.30: When morning came, they looked up and saw an innumerable multitude carrying ladders and machines to seize the fortress and fighting the Jews.
5.31: Seeing that the battle had begun and that the cries of the inhabitants rose to heaven with the sound of trumpets and great shouts, Judas
5.32: He told the men of his army: "Fight today for your brothers."«
5.33: And they advanced in three corps to the rear of the enemy, then they sounded the trumpets and prayed with loud cries.
5.34: As soon as Timothy's army recognized that it was Maccabeus, they fled before him and he inflicted a bloody defeat upon them; nearly eight thousand of their men perished that day.
5.35: From there, Judas turned towards Maspha, attacked it, seized it, killed all the male population, took their plunder and delivered the city to the flames.
5.36: Advancing further, he captured Casphon, Maced, Bosor and the other cities of Gileadite.
5.37: After these events, Timothy gathered another army and went to camp opposite Raphon, beyond the stream.
5.38: Judas sent to reconnoiter this army and received this report: "All the nations around us have joined Timothy's troops and form a very large army.
5.39: They bribed some Arabs as auxiliaries and set up camp beyond the stream, ready to give you battle.» And Judas advanced to meet them.
5.40: Timothy said to the commanders of his army, «When Judas and his troops approach the stream, if he goes over to us first, you will not be able to resist him; he will prevail over us.
5.41: But if he is afraid to cross and sets up camp beyond the river, let us cross over to him and prevail against him.»
5.42: Judas, having arrived at the river, had the army's scribes stop on the bank and gave them this order: "Let no one halt, but let all come to the battle."«
5.43: And, marching towards the enemy, he crossed the water first, followed by all the people. All the pagans were crushed before him; they threw down all their weapons and fled to the temple that is at Carnaim.
5.44: The Jews seized the city, burned the temple with all those who were in it, and Carnaim was brought low, and the enemies could no longer stand against Judas.
5.45: Then Judah gathered all the Israelites who were in Gilead, from the least to the greatest, with their wives, their children, and their possessions, an immense multitude, to bring them into the land of Judah.
5.46: They arrived at Ephron, a large city commanding the entrance to the country and very fortified; one could not turn away from it to the right or to the left, but it was necessary to cross it.
5.47: The inhabitants locked themselves in and blocked the gates with stones. Judas sent word of peace to them:
5.48: «"We want to cross your territory to get to our country, no one will harm you, we only ask to pass through." But they refused to open the gate for him.
5.49: Then Judas had it proclaimed throughout his army that everyone should take up their positions where they were.
5.50: The army men took up their positions, then he assaulted the city all day and all night, and the city was delivered into his hands.
5.51: He put all the males to the sword, destroyed the city from top to bottom, took away the loot and crossed it over the corpses.
5.52: Then, crossing the Jordan, the Jews arrived in the great plain opposite Bethsan.
5.53: Judas stood at the rear guard, rallying the stragglers and exhorting the people all the way, until they arrived in the land of Judah.
5.54: And they went up to Mount Zion with joy and gladness and offered burnt offerings, because they had returned safely, without losing any of their number.
5.55: While Judas was with Jonathan in the land of Gilead, and Simon, his brother, was in Galilee before Ptolemais,
5.56: Joseph, son of Zechariah, and Azariah, commanders of the army, heard of the glorious deeds they had performed and the battles they had fought,
5.57: And they said to themselves, "Let us also make a name for ourselves and go and fight against the nations around us."«
5.58: So they gave their orders to the men of their army and they marched against Jamnia.
5.59: Gorgias left the city with his men and advanced to meet them in battle.
5.60: Joseph and Azariah were defeated and pursued to the border of Judah; two thousand men of the people of Israel perished that day. This great defeat befell the people of Israel.
5.61: because they had not listened to Judas and his brothers, imagining they were showing valor.
5.62: But they were not of the race of those men into whose hands the salvation of Israel was entrusted.
5.63: The valiant Judah and his brothers had great glory before all Israel and all the nations where their name was spoken.
5.64: People gathered around them to congratulate them.
5.65: Then Judah set out with his brothers to fight the sons of Esau in the southern land, he seized Hebron and the cities of its dependencies, destroyed its fortifications and burned the towers of its enclosure.
5.66: Having broken camp, he went into the land of the Philistines and passed through Maresah.
5.67: On that day several priests perished in the battle who wanted to show bravery by imprudently taking part in the fight.
5.68: Then Judah went to Azotus, the territory of the Philistines, he demolished their altars, burned the carved images of their gods and, after plundering the cities, returned to the land of Judah.

6.1: However, King Antiochus was traveling through the high provinces. Having learned that there was a city in Persia, in Elymaide, famous for its wealth in silver and gold,
6.2: with a very rich temple containing golden armor, breastplates and other weapons left there by Alexander, son of Philip, king of Macedonia, who first reigned over the Greeks,
6.3: He went there and sought to take the city and plunder it, but he did not succeed, because the inhabitants of the city learned of his plan.
6.4: They rose up to fight him, and he fled and withdrew with great sadness, to return to Babylon.
6.5: Then a messenger came to Persia and announced the defeat of the troops that had entered the land of Judah:
6.6: Lysias, having advanced with a very strong army, had been forced to flee before the Jews, who had increased their power in arms, soldiers, and booty taken from the defeated armies.,
6.7: They had destroyed the abomination he had erected on the altar in Jerusalem, and they had surrounded the temple with high walls, as it had been before, and did the same in Beth-sur, one of his cities.
6.8: Upon hearing this news, the king was struck with terror, a great turmoil seized him, he threw himself on his bed and fell ill with sadness, because his desires had not been fulfilled.
6.9: He remained there for several days, constantly falling back into his deep melancholy. When he thought he was about to die,
6.10: He called his friends and said to them, "Sleep has departed from my eyes and grief makes my heart faint.
6.11: I say to myself: To what degree of affliction have I fallen and in what deep abyss am I now. I, who was good and loved in my kingdom.
6.12: But now I remember the evil I did in Jerusalem, I carried off all the gold and silver utensils that were there, and I sent an army to exterminate all the inhabitants of Judea without cause.
6.13: I therefore acknowledge that it is because of this that these afflictions have befallen me, and now I die in great sorrow in a foreign land.»
6.14: So he called Philip, one of his friends, and installed him over his entire kingdom.
6.15: He gave him his diadem, his robe and the royal seal, charging him with instructing his son Antiochus and raising him for kingship.
6.16: And King Antiochus died in this place, in the year one hundred and forty-nine.
6.17: When Lysias learned of the king's death, he appointed his son Antiochus, whom he had raised since childhood, to reign in his place and gave him the name Eupator.
6.18: The garrison of the citadel kept Israel confined around the sanctuary, constantly seeking to harass them, and was a support to the nations.
6.19: Judas resolved to destroy it and gathered all the people to besiege it.
6.20: They all gathered together, laid siege to it in the year one hundred and fifty, and built ballista towers and machines against it.
6.21: But some of the besieged escaped, and several ungodly Israelites joined them.
6.22: They went to the king and said to him, "How long will you delay in granting us justice and avenging our brothers?
6.23: We willingly placed ourselves at your father's service, doing what he told us and carrying out his orders.
6.24: Because of this, the sons of our people have become our enemies; all of us who fell into their hands were massacred, and they plundered our inheritances.
6.25: They have not only extended their hand over us, but over all the neighboring countries.
6.26: Look, they are currently encamped in front of the citadel of Jerusalem to seize it, and they have fortified the temple and Beth-sur.
6.27: If you don't hurry to warn them, they'll do even more and you won't be able to stop them.»
6.28: The king, having heard them, was seized with anger; he summoned all his friends, the leaders of his army, and those who commanded the cavalry.
6.29: He also received mercenary troops from other kingdoms and islands of the sea.
6.30: His army consisted of one hundred thousand infantrymen, twenty thousand cavalrymen and thirty-two elephants trained for war.
6.31: They advanced through Idumea and set up camp before Bethsur, they fought for a long time and built machines, but the Jews made a sortie and burned them, displaying great valor.
6.32: So Judas left the citadel and went to camp at Beth-Zachariah, opposite the king's camp.
6.33: The king got up early in the morning and suddenly ordered his army to march towards Beth-Zachariah, and the troops prepared for the attack and sounded the trumpet.
6.34: They placed grape and blackberry juice in front of the elephants to incite them to fight.
6.35: They distributed these animals among the phalanxes, each elephant was accompanied by a thousand men wearing iron mail cuirasses, with a bronze helmet on their heads, and five hundred elite horsemen were lined up beside it.
6.36: These men, in advance, were everywhere the beast was, wherever it went, they went there and they never left it.
6.37: On each of the elephants was erected, for its defense, a solid wooden tower attached around it by straps, and each animal carried thirty-two men of the army, fighting on the towers, in addition to its mahout.
6.38: They placed the rest of the cavalry on both flanks of the army, in order to harass the enemy and protect the phalanxes.
6.39: When the sun's rays fell upon the shields of gold and bronze, the mountains shone with their brilliance and shone like lamps of fire.
6.40: Part of the king's army deployed on the high mountains and the other part in the valleys, and they advanced with a sure step and in good order.
6.41: They were all terrified by the cries of this multitude, the noise of their marching, and the clash of their weapons. It was indeed an extremely large and powerful army.
6.42: Judas advanced with his army to give battle and six hundred men of the king's army fell.
6.43: Eleazar, nicknamed Abaron, spotted one of the elephants covered in royal harnesses and towering above all the others. Imagining that the king was upon it,
6.44: He dedicated himself to liberating his people and acquiring an immortal name.
6.45: He ran boldly towards him through the phalanx, killing to the right and left, and before him the enemies parted on both sides.
6.46: So he slipped under the elephant, thrust his sword into it and killed it; the elephant fell to the ground on top of him and Eleazar died there.
6.47: The Jews, seeing the strength of the kingdom and the impetuosity of the troops, withdrew before them.
6.48: At the same time, those of the king's army went up towards Jerusalem to meet the Jews, and the king established his camp against Judea and against Mount Zion.
6.49: He made peace with those who were at Bethsur and they left the city, because there had been no food to store for them in the place, for it was the year of the earth's rest.
6.50: The king thus seized Bethsur and left a garrison there to guard it.
6.51: He set up camp in front of the holy place for many days and erected ballista towers, war machines, catapults for launching flaming arrows and stones, scorpions for launching arrows and slings.
6.52: The besieged also built machines to oppose those of the besiegers and prolonged the resistance for a long time.
6.53: But there was no food in the storehouses, because it was the seventh year and the Israelites who had taken refuge in Judea from the nations had consumed the rest of what had been put in reserve.
6.54: Only a small number of Jews remained in the holy place, for the hunger was becoming more and more intense, and the others dispersed, each to their own home.
6.55: However, Philip, whom the still-living King Antiochus had designated to raise his son Antiochus and make him king,
6.56: had returned from Persia and Media, and with him the troops that had accompanied the king, and he sought to take control of the affairs of the kingdom.
6.57: At this news, Lysias had no more pressing need than to withdraw; he said to the king, the army commanders, and the troops: «We are diminishing here day by day, we have little food, and the place we are besieging is well fortified, and we have to attend to the affairs of state.
6.58: Now then, let us extend our hands to these men and make peace with them and with their entire nation.
6.59: Let us acknowledge their right to live according to their laws, as before, for it is because of these laws, which we have sought to abolish, that they became angry and did all this.»
6.60: This speech pleased the king and the chiefs, and he sent to them to negotiate peace, and they accepted it.
6.61: The king and the chiefs confirmed the treaty by oath, thereupon the besieged left the fortress.
6.62: But when the king entered the enclosure of Mount Zion and saw the fortifications, he violated the oath he had sworn and gave the order to destroy the walls all around.
6.63: Then he left in great haste and returned to Antioch, where he found Philip master of the city, he fought against him and took control of the city.

7.1: In the year one hundred and fifty-one, Demetrius, son of Seleucus, escaped from the city of Rome and landed, with a small number of people, in a seaside city where he took the title of king.
7.2: As soon as he had entered the kingdom of his fathers, the army seized Antiochus and Lysias to bring them to him.
7.3: When he was informed of this, he said, "Do not show me their faces."«
7.4: So the army killed them and Demetrius sat on the throne of his kingdom.
7.5: Then all the wicked and impious men of Israel came to him, led by Alcimus, who wanted to be high priest.
7.6: They accused the people to the king, saying, "Judas and his brothers have killed all your friends and expelled us from our land.
7.7: "Now send a man you trust to investigate all the destruction they have caused among us and in the king's provinces, and to punish the guilty along with all those who aid them."»
7.8: The king chose from among his friends Bacchides, governor of the country beyond the river, a very important man in the kingdom and loyal to the king,
7.9: and he sent him with the impious Alcimus, to whom he granted the high priesthood and ordered him to take revenge on the children of Israel.
7.10: Having set out, they came with a large army to the land of Judah and they sent messengers to bring Judah and his brothers words of peace, to deceive them.
7.11: But when they saw that they had come with a large army, they did not listen to their speech.
7.12: However, a group of scribes went to Alcimus and Bacchides to seek justice,
7.13: And those who held the first rank among the children of Israel, the Hasideans, asked them for peace,
7.14: For they were saying, "A priest of Aaron's line has come with the army; he cannot possibly mistreat us."«
7.15: He spoke words of peace to them and made this promise: "We do not wish to harm you or your friends."«
7.16: They believed him, but he seized sixty of them and had them massacred that same day, according to the word of Scripture:
7.17: «"They have scattered the flesh and shed the blood of your saints around Jerusalem, and there is no one to bury them."»
7.18: Then fear and terror seized all the people: "There is no longer," they said, "truth or justice among them, for they have violated their covenant and the oath they had taken."«
7.19: Bacchides left Jerusalem and went to camp at Bezeth, there he sent to seize a large number of those who had deserted his party, along with some of the people, and having killed them, he threw their corpses into the great cistern.
7.20: After entrusting the country to Alcime, leaving him troops to defend it, Bacchidès returned to the king.
7.21: Alcimus strove to seize the pontificate.
7.22: All those who were troubling their people gathered around him, took control of the land of Judah, and caused great affliction in Israel.
7.23: Seeing all the harm that Alcimus and his followers were doing to the children of Israel, more disastrous than the pagans themselves,
7.24: Judas traveled throughout the territory of Judea, punishing the apostates and preventing them from spreading into the countryside.
7.25: When Alcimus saw that Judas and his companions had become powerful, recognizing that he could not stand against them, he returned to the king and accused them of the greatest misdeeds.
7.26: The king sent Nicanor, one of his most illustrious generals, filled with hatred and animosity towards Israel, with orders to exterminate the people.
7.27: Having arrived in Jerusalem with a large army, Nicanor sent words of peace to Judas and his brothers, in order to deceive them:
7.28: «"Let there be no war between you and me," he said. "I want to go with a small number of men to see your faces in friendship."»
7.29: So he came to Judas and they greeted each other with friendly displays, but the enemies were ready to seize Judas.
7.30: Having learned that Nicanor had come to him with a treacherous purpose, Judas, frightened, withdrew and refused to see him again.
7.31: Nicanor then recognized that his plan had been discovered and he immediately took up arms against Judas near Capharsalama.
7.32: About five thousand men of Nicanor's army were killed, the rest fled to the city of David.
7.33: After these events, Nicanor having gone up to Mount Zion, some of the priests came out of the holy place, accompanied by several elders of the people, to greet him amicably and show him the burnt offerings that were offered for the king.
7.34: But he, mocking them and treating them with contempt, defiled them and uttered insolent words.
7.35: And he angrily swore this oath: "If Judas and his army are not handed over to me at once, as soon as I return to peace, I will burn this building down." And he went out in a rage.
7.36: Then the priests returned and, standing before the altar and the sanctuary, they said, weeping:
7.37: «It is you, Lord, who chose this house to put your name on, so that it might be for your people a house of prayer and supplication.
7.38: "Take vengeance on this man and his army, and let them fall by the sword. Remember their blasphemies and do not allow them to remain."»
7.39: Nicanor, leaving Jerusalem, went to camp near Bethoron and a body of Syrians came to meet him.
7.40: Judas, for his part, camped near Adasa with three thousand men and he prayed, saying:
7.41: «"Those who had been sent by the king of the Assyrians having blasphemed you, Lord, your angel came and killed one hundred and eighty-five thousand of them.
7.42: "Exterminate this army in our presence today, so that all the others may recognize that he has spoken an impious language against your sanctuary, and judge him according to his wickedness."»
7.43: The armies clashed on the thirteenth day of the month of Adar and Nicanor's troops were cut to pieces, he himself being the first to fall in the battle.
7.44: The troops, seeing that Nicanor had fallen, threw down their weapons and fled.
7.45: The Jews pursued them for a day's journey, from Adasa to the vicinity of Gazara, sounding trumpets behind them in fanfare.
7.46: From all the surrounding villages of Judea came people who surrounded the Syrians: then they turned on each other and all fell by the sword, without any of them escaping, not even one.
7.47: They took the spoils of the vanquished, as well as their own spoils, and having cut off Nicanor's head and his right hand, which he had insolently extended, they brought them and hung them up in sight of Jerusalem.
7.48: The people were filled with joy and they celebrated this day as a day of great gladness.
7.49: It was decided that this day would be celebrated every year, on the thirteenth of the month of Adar.
7.50: And the land of Judah was at peace for a while.

8.1: Now Judas heard about the Romans: they are, he was told, powerful in battles, they show kindness to all those who attach themselves to their cause and make friends with anyone who comes to them, and they are powerful in battles.
8.2: They told him about their wars and the exploits they had accomplished among the Galatians, whom they had subjugated and made tributaries,
8.3: all that they had done in the country of Spain, to seize the gold and silver mines located there, and how they had subdued the entire country through their prudence and patience:
8.4: This country was very far from them. The same had been true of the kings who had come to attack them from the ends of the earth; they had defeated them and struck them with a great plague, and the others pay them an annual tribute.
8.5: They had defeated Philip and Perseus, king of the Cetheans, and those who had taken up arms against them in the war, and they had subdued them.
8.6: Antiochus the Great, king of Asia, who had advanced against them to fight them with one hundred and twenty elephants, cavalry, chariots and a very powerful army, had also been defeated by them,
8.7: They had taken him alive and forced him to pay them and his successors a considerable tribute, to hand over hostages, and to cede part of his kingdom.,
8.8: to know the country of India, Media and Lydia and portions of its most beautiful provinces and, after having received them from him, they had ceded them to King Eumenes.
8.9: Those in Greece who had plotted to destroy them, the Romans had learned of it.
8.10: and had sent against them a single general, they had waged war against them, killed a great number of them, taken their wives and children captive, plundered their possessions, subjugated their country, destroyed their fortresses and reduced the inhabitants to servitude to this day.
8.11: All the other kingdoms and islands that had resisted them, they had destroyed and subjugated.
8.12: But to their friends and those who trust them, they maintain friendship; they have become masters of kingdoms near and far, and all who hear their name fear them.
8.13: They give aid to all those they wish to help and confer kingship upon, and they take power away from whomever they please; it is a very powerful nation.
8.14: Despite all this, none of them wears the diadem, none dresses in purple to elevate themselves in this way.
8.15: They formed a senate, where three hundred and twenty members deliberate every day, constantly concerned with the interests of the people, to make them prosperous.
8.16: Each year they entrust power to a single man to rule throughout their country; all obey this one man, and there is neither envy nor jealousy among them.
8.17: Judas chose Eupolemus, son of John, son of Accos, and Jason, son of Eleazar, and sent them to Rome to make a friendship and alliance with them.,
8.18: and so that they might deliver them from the yoke, for they saw that the kingdom of the Greeks was reducing Israel to servitude.
8.19: So they went to Rome, and the journey was very long, and having entered the Senate, they spoke in these terms:
8.20: «Judas Maccabeus, his brothers, and the Jewish people have sent us to you to conclude a treaty of alliance and peace with you, and to be counted among your allies and friends.»
8.22: And here is the copy of the treaty that the Romans engraved on bronze tablets and sent to Jerusalem, to remain there as a monument of peace and alliance:
8.23: «"Prosperity to the Romans and to the Jewish nation on sea and on land, forever. Far from them the sword and the enemy.".
8.24: If war breaks out first against the Romans, or against one of their allies throughout their empire,
8.25: The Jewish nation will wholeheartedly lend them aid, as circumstances permit.,
8.26: They will give nothing to the fighters and will provide neither grain, nor weapons, nor money, nor ships. This is the will of the Romans, and the Jews will keep their promises without receiving anything.
8.27: Likewise, if war breaks out against the Jewish nation first, the Romans will fight alongside them with all their might, to the best of their ability, as circumstances permit.,
8.28: without the auxiliary troops being supplied with grain, weapons, money, or ships. This is Rome's will, and they will keep their promises without deceit.
8.29: These are the clauses of the treaty between the Romans and the Jewish people.
8.30: If, in the future, either party wishes to add to or subtract from it, they may do so as they see fit, and whatever has been added or subtracted will be binding.»
8.31: «Regarding the harm that King Demetrius has done to them, we wrote to him in these terms: «Why do you place the yoke upon the Jews, who are our friends and allies?
8.32: »If they accuse you again to us, we will uphold their rights and fight you on land and sea.”

9.1: Having learned that Nicanor and his army had fallen in battle, Demetrius once again sent Bacchides and Alcimus to Judea, with the right wing of his army.
9.2: They took the road that leads to Galgala and set up camp at Masaloth, which is in the territory of Arbela, they captured this city and killed a large number of inhabitants.
9.3: In the first month of the year one hundred and fifty-two, they arrayed their troops before Jerusalem.
9.4: Then they broke camp and went to Berea with twenty thousand men and two thousand horsemen.
9.5: Judas had established his camp at Eleasah, with three thousand elite warriors with him.
9.6: At the sight of the large number of enemies, they were filled with fear and many secretly fled from the camp; only eight hundred remained.
9.7: Judas saw that his army had fled and yet the battle was imminent, so his heart was broken, because he lacked the time to gather his people and he felt himself faint.
9.8: However, he said to those who remained with him: "Come, let us march against our adversaries, if indeed we can fight them."«
9.9: But they dissuaded him, saying, "We cannot do that; let us save our lives now and return to our brothers. Then we will come back to fight our enemies, but we are too few."«
9.10: Judas said to them, "Far be it from me to act like that, to flee from them. If our time has come, let us die bravely for our brothers and leave no stain on our glory."»
9.11: The Syrian army came out of the camp, advancing to meet them, the horsemen were divided into two corps, the slingers and archers marched in the front, the bravest in the first rank.
9.12: Bacchides was on the right wing and the phalanx advanced on both sides, to the sound of the trumpet.
9.13: Those on Judas' side also sounded their trumpets, and the earth shook with the sound of the two armies. The battle began and lasted from morning until evening.
9.14: Judas, seeing that Bacchides and his best troops were on the right wing, gathered around him all the most ardent men,
9.15: defeated the Syrians' right wing and pursued them as far as Mount Azot.
9.16: But those on the left wing, realizing that the right wing had been defeated, turned around and followed Judas and his men from behind.,
9.17: The fighting became fierce and there were a great many deaths on both sides.
9.18: Judas also fell and his companions fled.
9.19: Jonathan and Simon took Judas, their brother, and buried him in the tomb of their fathers at Modin.
9.20: All Israel wept for him and made great lamentations over him; they mourned for several days.,
9.21: And people were saying, "How the hero has fallen, the one who saved Israel!"«
9.22: The rest of Judas's story, his other wars, his other exploits and his titles of glory have not been written down, for they are very numerous.
9.23: After Judas' death, the wicked appeared throughout the territory of Israel, and all who commit iniquity raised their heads.
9.24: In those days, a very great famine occurred, and the country rallied to them.
9.25: Bacchides chose the impious men and established them to administer the country.
9.26: They searched for Judas' friends and, when they found some, they brought them to Bacchides, who punished them and ridiculed them.
9.27: And Israel was afflicted with great tribulation, such as had not been since the day that no prophet arose in Israel.
9.28: Then all of Judas' friends gathered together and said to Jonathan:
9.29: «"Since your brother Judas died, there is no longer a man like him to march against our enemies, the Bacchides and all those who hate our nation.
9.30: Therefore, we choose you today to be our leader in his place and to command us in our battles.»
9.31: Jonathan therefore received the command at that time and he stood up in place of Judah, his brother.
9.32: As soon as Bacchides learned of Jonathan's election, he sought to have him killed.
9.33: Having been informed of this plan, Jonathan, his brother Simon, and all those who were with him fled to the desert of Tekoa and settled near the waters of the cistern of Asphar.
9.34: Bacchides learned of this on the Sabbath day and he himself went with his entire army beyond the Jordan.
9.35: Jonathan sent his brother John, as leader of the people, to the Nabataeans, his friends, asking them to allow them to store his luggage, which was considerable.
9.36: But the sons of Jambri, having come out of Madaba, seized John and all his belongings and departed with all this plunder.
9.37: Some time later, Jonathan and his brother Simon were told that the sons of Jambri were celebrating a solemn wedding and that they were bringing the bride, daughter of one of the powerful princes of Canaan, from Nadabat in great pomp.
9.38: Then, remembering their brother John, they climbed up and hid in the shelter of the mountain.
9.39: Looking up, they watched, and behold, a great noise was heard and a large procession appeared; the bridegroom, accompanied by his brothers and friends, advanced to meet them, with tambourines, musical instruments and considerable equipment.
9.40: At this sight, Jonathan's companions rose from their ambush and rushed upon them to massacre them; a great number fell under their blows, the rest fled into the mountains, and the Jews seized their plunder.
9.41: Thus the wedding turned into mourning, and the joyful sounds of their music into lamentation.
9.42: Having thus avenged the murder of their brother, Jonathan and Simon withdrew to the marshes of the Jordan.
9.43: Bacchides was informed of this and he came on the Sabbath day to the banks of the Jordan with a powerful army.
9.44: Then Jonathan said to his companions: «Let us now rise and fight for our lives. For today is not like yesterday and the day before yesterday.
9.45: Here is the enemy in arms before us and behind us and on all sides the water of the Jordan, a marsh and a wood, no way to escape.
9.46: "Now therefore cry out to heaven, that you may be saved from the hand of your enemies." The battle began.
9.47: Jonathan stretched out his hand to strike Bacchides, but the latter, to avoid it, threw himself backwards.
9.48: So Jonathan jumped into the Jordan, with his companions, they swam across and the Syrians did not cross to pursue them.
9.49: A thousand men perished that day on the side of Bacchides. He returned to Jerusalem,
9.50: and built fortified cities in Judea, the fortress near Jericho, Emmaus, Bethoron, Bethel, Thamnatha, Pharaoh, and Tephon, with high walls, gates, and bars,
9.51: and he placed garrisons there to wage hostilities against Israel.
9.52: He fortified the city of Bethsur, Gazara and the citadel and placed troops and food stores there.
9.53: He took the sons of the leading men of the country hostage and held them prisoner in the citadel of Jerusalem.
9.54: In the year one hundred and fifty-three, in the second month, Alcimus ordered the walls of the inner courtyard of the sanctuary to be torn down, thus destroying the work of the prophets, and he began to demolish them.
9.55: At that time, Alcime was struck by God and his enterprises were stopped, his mouth was closed, afflicted with paralysis, he could no longer utter a single word, nor give any order concerning the affairs of his house.
9.56: And Alcimus died at that time under great torture.
9.57: Seeing that Alcimus was dead, Bacchides returned to the king and the land of Judah was at peace for two years.
9.58: Then all the unbelieving Jews held council, saying, "Now Jonathan and his companions are living in peace and security, let us bring Bacchides and he will capture them all in one night."«
9.59: And they went to come to an agreement with him.
9.60: Bacchides set out at the head of a large army and secretly sent letters to all his supporters in Judea, asking them to seize Jonathan and his companions, but they did not succeed because the latter became aware of their plan.
9.61: And among the men of the country, leaders of the conspiracy, they took fifty and put them to death.
9.62: Then Jonathan, with Simon and those who were with them, went to Bethbasi in the desert and repaired its ruins and fortified it.
9.63: Bacchides learned of this, gathered all his troops and called upon his supporters in Judea.
9.64: He came and set up camp near Bethbasi, he besieged that city for many days and built machines.
9.65: But Jonathan, leaving his brother Simon in the city, went out into the countryside and returned with a small group.
9.66: He defeated Odoare, as well as his brothers and the sons of Phaseron in their tents, and he began to attack the besiegers and march against them with forces.
9.67: Simon, for his part, went out with his companions and burned the war machines.
9.68: They both fought against Bacchides, routed him, and plunged him into deep distress because his plan and expedition had completely failed.
9.69: Enraged with anger against the impious men who had advised him to come to the country, he killed a great number of them and resolved to return to his own country.
9.70: Jonathan knew about this and sent messengers to negotiate peace with him and obtain the return of the prisoners.
9.71: Bacchidès welcomed them and accepted their proposals; he swore an oath to Jonathan not to harm him as long as he lived.
9.72: He returned to him the prisoners whom he had previously taken in the land of Judah, and having gone to his own country, he did not return to the territory of the Jews.
9.73: The sword rested in Israel, and Jonathan settled in Machmas, and he began to judge the people and he removed the wicked from the midst of Israel.

10.1: In the year one hundred and sixty, Alexander, son of Antiochus and nicknamed Epiphanes, set out and seized Ptolemais, the inhabitants received him and he became king.
10.2: King Demetrius, having learned of this, assembled a very strong army and advanced against him to fight him.
10.3: At the same time, Demetrius sent Jonathan a letter with words of peace, promising to raise him in rank.
10.4: «"Let us hasten," he said, "to make peace with him, before he makes it with Alexander against us.".
10.5: For he will remember all the harm we did to him, to his brother, and to all his people.»
10.6: He authorized him to raise troops, manufacture weapons and call himself his ally, and he ordered that the hostages held in the citadel be handed over to him.
10.7: Jonathan immediately went to Jerusalem and read the letter before all the people and before those who were in the citadel.
10.8: They were seized with great fear when they learned that the king was giving Jonathan the power to raise an army.
10.9: Those in the citadel handed the hostages over to Jonathan, who returned them to their parents.
10.10: Jonathan settled in Jerusalem and began to rebuild and renew the city.
10.11: He ordered the workers to rebuild the walls and to surround Mount Zion with square stones to fortify it. These orders were carried out.
10.12: Then the foreigners who were in the fortresses that Bacchides had built fled,
10.13: and each of them, leaving their home, returned to their own country.
10.14: Only a few of those who had abandoned the law and the commandments remained in Bethsur, which became their refuge.
10.15: However, King Alexander learned of the offers that Demetrius had made to Jonathan, he was also told of the battles that Jonathan had fought, the exploits that he and his brothers had accomplished, as well as the evils that they had endured.
10.16: And he said, "Would we ever find such a man? Let us then make him our friend and ally."«
10.17: He wrote a letter and sent it to her, worded as follows:
10.18: «"King Alexander to his brother Jonathan: Greetings.".
10.19: We have learned that you are a powerful man and that you are willing to be our friend.
10.20: Therefore, we appoint you today as high priest of the nation and give you the title of friend of the king. He also sent him a purple robe and a golden crown. Take an interest in our affairs and maintain your friendship with us.»
10.21: Jonathan donned the sacred ornaments in the seventh month of the year one hundred and sixty, on the feast of Tabernacles, and he raised an army and made many weapons.
10.22: Upon learning these things, Demetrius felt great sorrow:
10.23: «"What have we done," he said, "that Alexander has warned us by obtaining the friendship of the Jews to strengthen his power?"
10.24: I too want to address them with persuasive words, offer them a high position and gifts, so that they will be my allies.»
10.25: So he sent them a letter worded as follows: "King Demetrius to the Jewish nation: Greetings.
10.26: You have faithfully kept the covenant made with us, persevering in our friendship and not joining our enemies; we have learned of this and we have rejoiced.
10.27: And now, continue to remain loyal to us and we will reward what you do for us with good deeds.
10.28: We will grant you many exemptions and favors.
10.29: From this moment on, I release you and grant remission to all the Jews of the taxes, the salt tax, and the crowns. This is due to me as one-third of the produce of the land.
10.30: and for half of the produce of the fruit trees, I grant you today a remission and I will no longer demand anything from the land of Judah, nor from the three cantons joined to it, Samaria and Galilee.
10.31: I want Jerusalem to be a holy and exempt city, along with its territory, tithes, and tribute.
10.32: I also relinquish my authority over the citadel which is in Jerusalem and I give it to the high priest so that he may appoint to guard it the men whom he shall choose.
10.33: All the Jews who were taken captive from the land of Judah throughout the extent of my kingdom, I am setting free without ransom, and I want everyone to also remit tribute to them, even for their animals.
10.34: Let all solemnities, Sabbaths, New Moons, appointed days, and the three days preceding or following a solemn feast, be days of immunity and freedom for all Jews who dwell in my kingdom.
10.35: In those days, no one will have the right to prosecute any of them, or to bring an action against them in any matter whatsoever.
10.36: Up to thirty thousand Jews will be enlisted in the king's army and given the same pay as all the king's troops. A number of them will be stationed in the king's major fortresses.,
10.37: and many will be admitted to positions of trust in the kingdom; moreover, these troops will have leaders taken from their ranks at their head and they will live according to their laws, as the king has ordered for the land of Judah.
10.38: The three cantons of Samaria annexed to Judea will be incorporated into it and counted as forming one with it, so that they will obey no other authority than that of the high priest.
10.39: I give Ptolemais and its territory to the sanctuary in Jerusalem, for the expenses necessary for worship.
10.40: And I give every year fifteen thousand shekels of silver, which will be taken from the royal treasury in suitable localities.
10.41: And any surplus that tax officials will not have paid as in previous years, they will settle in the future for the service of the temple.
10.42: In addition, the five thousand shekels of silver that the officers deducted each year from the needs of the sanctuary, by taking them from its revenues, will be remitted, because they belong to the priests who perform the service.
10.43: Whoever is a refugee in the sanctuary of Jerusalem and in all its enclosure, being liable for royal taxes or any other debt, shall be free, with all the property he possesses in my kingdom.
10.44: The expenses for the construction and restoration of the sanctuary will also be drawn from the king's revenues.
10.45: Furthermore, the expenses for rebuilding the walls of Jerusalem and strengthening its perimeter will again be drawn from the king's revenues, and the same will be true for rebuilding the walls of the cities of Judea.»
10.46: When Jonathan and the people heard these words, they did not believe them and refused to accept them, because they remembered the great evils that Demetrius had done to Israel and the calamities he had caused them.
10.47: They therefore decided in favor of Alexander, whose peaceful proposals were preferred in their eyes, and they were constantly his allies.
10.48: King Alexander assembled a large army and advanced against Demetrius.
10.49: The two kings having engaged in battle, Demetrius' army fled, and Alexander pursued them, ultimately defeating them.
10.50: and fought very valiantly until sunset, and Demetrius was killed that day.
10.51: Alexander sent ambassadors to Ptolemy, king of Egypt, with the message:
10.52: «"I have returned to my kingdom and I sit on the throne of my fathers, I have reconquered the government by my victory over Demetrius and I have taken possession of our country.
10.53: I gave him battle and he was defeated by me, him and his army, and I ascended the seat of his kingship.
10.54: Now let us become friends; give me your daughter in marriage, I will be your son-in-law, and I will give you and her gifts worthy of you.»
10.55: King Ptolemy replied in these terms: «Happy is the day you returned to the land of your fathers and sat down on the throne of their kingship.
10.56: Now I will do for you what you have written, but come to meet me at Ptolemais, so that we may see each other and I will make you my son-in-law, as you have expressed your desire.»
10.57: Ptolemy left Egypt, he and his daughter Cleopatra, and went to Ptolemais, in the year one hundred and sixty-two.
10.58: King Alexander came to meet him and he gave him his daughter Cleopatra and he celebrated the wedding at Ptolemais with great magnificence, according to the custom of kings.
10.59: King Alexander also wrote to Jonathan, inviting him to meet with him.
10.60: Jonathan went in great pomp to Ptolemais, where he met with the two kings, he offered them and their courtiers money, gold and many gifts and he won their favor.
10.61: Then wicked men of Israel, the impious, gathered together against him to accuse him, but the king did not listen to them.
10.62: The king even ordered that Jonathan's clothes be removed and that he be dressed in purple. This order having been carried out, the king had him sit beside him,
10.63: And he said to the nobles of his court, "Go out with him into the middle of the city and proclaim that no one should bring a complaint against him for anything and that no one should harass him under any pretext."«
10.64: When his accusers saw that he was being given these public honors and that he was clothed in purple, they all fled.
10.65: Adding further to these honors, the king included him among his closest friends and made him a general and provincial governor.
10.66: And Jonathan returned to Jerusalem in peace and joy.
10.67: In the year one hundred and sixty-five, Demetrius, son of Demetrius, came from Crete to the land of his fathers.
10.68: Upon hearing this news, King Alexander felt great sorrow and returned to Antioch.
10.69: Demetrius appointed Apollonius, governor of Coele-Syria, as his general, and Apollonius assembled a large army and encamped near Jamnia. There, he sent word to Jonathan, the high priest:
10.70: «"You, all alone, rise up against us, and I have become an object of derision and opprobrium because of you. How dare you, you, play the independent one against us, in your mountains?"
10.71: Now then, if you are confident in your strength, come down to us in the plain and there let us test ourselves together, for I have the powerful cities of the coast on my side.
10.72: Find out more and learn who I am and who the others are who support me. They say your foot cannot stand against us, since twice your ancestors were driven to flight in their own land.
10.73: And now, you will not be able to withstand the onslaught of the cavalry and such a large army on a plain where there is neither stone nor rock, nor any place to take refuge.»
10.74: When Jonathan heard Apollonius' words, he felt a strong indignation, he chose ten thousand men and left Jerusalem and his brother Simon came to join him to support him.
10.75: They went to camp near Joppa, the city closed its gates to them, because it was occupied by a garrison of Apollonius, so they began the siege.
10.76: The frightened inhabitants opened the gates and Jonathan became master of Joppa.
10.77: As soon as he was informed, Apollonius deployed three thousand cavalrymen and a large army in battle formation.,
10.78: and headed towards Azot, as if to retreat, and at the same time he advanced towards the plain, because he had a large number of horsemen in whom he had confidence. Jonathan followed him towards Azot and the two armies clashed.
10.79: Apollonius had left behind a thousand horsemen in a hidden outpost.,
10.80: But Jonathan learned that there was an ambush set behind him. The horsemen surrounded his troop and fired arrows at his men from morning until evening.
10.81: And his men held firm, as Jonathan had recommended, while the horsemen's horses grew tired.
10.82: Then Simon advanced his troops and attacked the phalanx, for the cavalry was without strength; the Syrians were defeated by him and fled.
10.83: The cavalry broke ranks across the plain and the fugitives reached Azot, where they entered Beth-Dagon, their idol temple, to seek refuge.
10.84: Jonathan burned Azot and the surrounding towns, after having plundered them, and he gave fire to the temple of Dagon with those who had taken refuge there.
10.85: The number of those who perished by the sword or were consumed by fire was about eight thousand.
10.86: And, departing from there, Jonathan came to camp near Ascalon, whose inhabitants came to meet him, paying him great honors.
10.87: Then Jonathan returned to Jerusalem with his people, having a rich spoil of war.
10.88: When King Alexander learned of these events, he bestowed further honors upon Jonathan.
10.89: He sent him a gold clasp, as is customary to reward the relatives of kings, and he gave him Accaron and its territory as his property.

11.1: The king of Egypt assembled an army as vast as the sand on the seashore and many ships, and he sought to take control of Alexander's kingdom by trickery and annex it to his kingdom.
11.2: So he advanced towards Syria with words of peace; the inhabitants of the cities opened their doors to him and rushed to meet him, for King Alexander had ordered them to go to meet him, because he was his father-in-law.
11.3: But, as soon as Ptolemy entered a city, he left some of his troops there to guard it.
11.4: When he approached Azot, the inhabitants showed him the burned temple of Dagon, the city itself and its surroundings in ruins, the scattered corpses and the remains of those who had been burned in the war, for they had made heaps of them on the road.
11.5: And they told the king what Jonathan had done, in order to make him odious, but the king remained silent.
11.6: Jonathan went to the king in Joppa to pay him homage; they greeted each other and spent the night there.
11.7: Jonathan accompanied the king to the river called Eleuthera, then he returned to Jerusalem.
11.8: King Ptolemy thus gained control of the maritime cities as far as Seleucia on the sea and he plotted evil designs against Alexander.
11.9: He sent ambassadors to King Demetrius, saying: "Come, let us make an alliance together and I will give you my daughter whom Alexander married and you will reign in your father's kingdom.
11.10: I regret giving him my daughter, because he tried to kill me.»
11.11: He belittled him like that because he coveted his kingdom.
11.12: Having abducted his daughter, he gave her to Demetrius, from then on he broke with Alexander and their hostility became public.
11.13: Ptolemy entered Antioch, took the diadem of Asia, thus placing two crowns on his head, that of Egypt and that of Asia.
11.14: At that time, Alexander was in Cilicia because the inhabitants of that region had revolted.
11.15: As soon as he learned of his stepfather's betrayal, Alexander advanced against him to fight him, but King Ptolemy deployed his army, marched to meet him with great forces and put him to flight.
11.16: Alexander fled to Arabia to seek asylum, and King Ptolemy triumphed.
11.17: The Arab Zabdiel cut off Alexander's head and sent it to Ptolemy.
11.18: King Ptolemy died three days later and the Egyptians who were in the fortresses were killed by their inhabitants.
11.19: And Demetrius became king in the year one hundred and sixty-seven.
11.20: In those days Jonathan gathered those who were in Judea in order to seize the citadel of Jerusalem and he set up many war machines against it.
11.21: Then some impious men, who hated their nation, went to King Demetrius and reported to him that Jonathan was besieging the citadel.
11.22: Upon hearing this story, Demetrius was angered; he hastened to Ptolemais and wrote to Jonathan to cease the siege of the citadel and to come immediately to him in Ptolemais to confer with him.
11.23: When Jonathan received this letter, he ordered the siege to continue and, having chosen to accompany him some elders of Israel and several priests, he exposed himself to danger.
11.24: Having taken with him gold, silver, clothing and many other gifts, he went to the king at Ptolemais and received a favorable reception from him.
11.25: Some wicked men of the nation lodged complaints against him.
11.26: But the king did for him what his predecessors had done: he showered him with honors, in the presence of all his friends,
11.27: confirmed his pontificate and all the distinctions he previously held, and had him enrolled among his closest friends.
11.28: Jonathan asked the king to exempt Judea and the three toparcies of Samaria from all tribute and promised him in return three hundred talents.
11.29: The king agreed and wrote Jonathan a letter about all this, worded as follows:
11.30: «"King Demetrius to his brother Jonathan and to the nation of the Jews: Greetings.
11.31: We are sending you a copy of the letter we wrote about you to Lasthenes, our cousin, so that you may be aware of it:
11.32: «"King Demetrius to Lasthenes, his father: Greetings.
