11.33: We have resolved to do good to the Jewish nation, who are our friends and observe what is right towards us, because of the good feelings they have shown us.
11.34: We confirm to them both the territory of Judea and the three cantons detached from Samaria to be united to Judea, namely Ephraim, Lydda and Ramathaim with all their dependencies, in favor of all those who go to sacrifice in Jerusalem we make this concession, in place of the dues which previously the king received from them each year on the productions of the soil and the fruits of the trees.
11.35: And all other rights belonging to us, from this day forward, whether on the tithes and tributes that belong to us, or on the salt marshes and crowns that were due to us,
11.36: We are granting them a full remission. None of these favors will be revoked from now on, under any circumstances.
11.37: "Now then, take care to make a copy of this decree and give it to Jonathan and place it on the holy mountain in a visible location."»
11.38: King Demetrius, seeing that the country was at peace before him and that he no longer had to overcome any resistance, sent all his army back to their homes, with the exception of the foreign troops he had recruited from the islands of the nations, and thus all the armies of his fathers became his enemies.
11.39: Tryphon, who had previously been one of Alexander's supporters, seeing that the whole army was murmuring against Demetrius, went to find the Arab Emalchuel, who was raising Antiochus, Alexander's young son.
11.40: He pressed him to hand him over, in order to make him reign in place of his father, he told him everything that Demetrius had done and the hatred of his troops against him and he remained there for a great many days.
11.41: Jonathan sent word to King Demetrius to withdraw the troops that were in the citadel of Jerusalem and in the other fortresses of Judea, because they were waging war against Israel.
11.42: Demetrius replied to Jonathan: "I will not only do this for you and your nation, but I want to shower you and your nation with honors as soon as circumstances permit.
11.43: Now, therefore, you would do well to send men to my aid, for my entire army has deserted.»
11.44: Jonathan sent three thousand of his most valiant men to Antioch; they went to the king, who rejoiced at their arrival.
11.45: The inhabitants of the city gathered within the city itself, numbering one hundred and twenty thousand, wanting to kill the king.
11.46: With the king having taken refuge in the palace, the inhabitants occupied the streets of the city and began to fight.
11.47: Then the king called upon the Jews for help; they all gathered around him and then spread throughout the city, killing approximately one hundred thousand men that day.,
11.48: They burned the city, made considerable plunder that day, and freed the king.
11.49: Seeing that the Jews held the city at their mercy, the inhabitants lost heart and made pleading cries to the king:
11.50: «"Grant us peace and let the Jews cease fighting against us and against the city."»
11.51: At the same time, they laid down their weapons and made peace. The Jews gained much glory in the sight of the king and all who were in his kingdom, and they returned to Jerusalem with rich spoils.
11.52: King Demetrius was able to sit on the throne of his kingdom and the country was at peace before him.
11.53: But he denied all the promises he had made, he distanced himself from Jonathan and did not realize the benevolent intentions he had shown him, and he grieved him greatly.
11.54: After that, Tryphon returned, bringing with him Antiochus, a young child, and he proclaimed him king and placed the diadem on him.
11.55: Around him gathered all the troops that Demetrius had dismissed; they fought against him, and he fled and was defeated.
11.56: Tryphon seized the elephants and occupied Antioch.
11.57: Then the young Antiochus wrote Jonathan a letter worded as follows: "I confirm you in the priesthood and establish you over the four territories and give you the rank of friend of the king."«
11.58: He also sent her gold vessels and a dinner service, with permission to drink from a gold cup, to dress in purple, and to wear a gold clasp.
11.59: And he appointed Simon, his brother, governor of the land which extends from the Ladder of Tyre to the border of Egypt.
11.60: Then Jonathan went out and began to travel through the land beyond the river, and all the Syrian troops gathered around him to fight alongside him. He came to Ashkelon, whose inhabitants came out to meet him, paying him great honors.
11.61: From there, he went to Gaza. The inhabitants having closed their gates to him, he besieged the city, burned the surrounding area and looted it.
11.62: Then the people of Gaza begged Jonathan, and he granted them peace, but he took the sons of their leaders hostage and sent them to Jerusalem. He traveled through the region as far as Damascus.
11.63: Jonathan then learned that Demetrius' generals were at Cades in Galilee, at the head of a large army, with the intention of diverting him from his undertaking.
11.64: He marched against them, after leaving his brother Simon in the country.
11.65: Simon advanced towards Bethsura, besieged it for many days and surrounded it.
11.66: When the besieged asked him for peace, he granted it, made them leave the city, took possession of it and placed a garrison there.
11.67: Jonathan and his army camped near the waters of Gennesar and the next day, at dawn, they entered the plain of Asor.
11.68: And behold, foreign troops were advancing to meet him in the plain, having set up an ambush against him in the mountains, and they marched straight to meet him.
11.69: Suddenly the men in the ambush came out of their hiding place and engaged in combat, and Jonathan's people all fled.
11.70: No one remained except Mathathias, son of Absalom, and Judas, son of Calphi, generals of the troops.
11.71: Then Jonathan tore his clothes, put dust on his head, and prayed,
11.72: Then he turned against them in battle, made them retreat and put them to flight.
11.73: At this sight, those of his people who were fleeing returned to him and together they pursued the enemy to Cades, where his camp was, and they themselves camped there.
11.74: Three thousand men of foreign troops perished that day, and Jonathan returned to Jerusalem.

12.1: Jonathan, seeing that the circumstances were favorable, chose men and sent them to Rome to confirm and renew the friendship of the Jews with the Romans.
12.2: He also sent letters to the Spartans and elsewhere along the same lines.
12.3: So they went to Rome, entered the Senate, and said, "Jonathas the High Priest and the Jewish nation have sent us to renew the friendship and alliance with them, as it existed before."«
12.4: And the senate delivered a letter to the Roman authorities in each place, recommending that they arrange a safe return to the land of Judah.
12.5: Here is a copy of the letter that Jonathan wrote to the Spartans:
12.6: «"Jonathas, high priest, the senate of the nation, the priests and the rest of the Jewish people, to the Spartans their brothers: greetings.
12.7: Already, in times past, a letter was sent to Onias, high priest, from Areius who reigned over you, attesting that you are our brothers, as evidenced by the copy below.
12.8: Onias honorably received the man who was sent and received the letter in which alliance and friendship were clearly spoken of.
12.9: Therefore, although we have no need of these things, having for consolation the holy Books which are in our hands,
12.10: We have tried to send to you to renew the brotherhood and friendship that unites us with you, so that we do not become strangers to you, for many years have passed since you sent to us.
12.11: Therefore, we constantly remember you at all times, both in our solemnities and on other holy days, in the sacrifices we offer and in our prayers, as is right and proper to remember one's brothers.
12.13: But we are besieged by numerous calamities and incessant wars; the kings who surround us are waging war against us.
12.14: We did not want, during these wars, to be a burden either to you or to our other allies and friends.
12.15: For we have heavenly help to assist us, and we have been delivered, and our enemies have been humbled.
12.16: That is why we chose Numenius, son of Antiochus, and Antipater, son of Jason, and sent them to the Romans to renew with them the ancient friendship and alliance.
12.17: We therefore instructed them to also come to you, to greet you and to bring you our letter concerning the renewal of our brotherhood.
12.18: And now you would do well to answer us on this matter.»
12.19: Here is a copy of the letter we sent to Onias:
12.20: «"Areius, king of the Spartans, to the high priest Onias: greetings.
12.21: It was found in a writing about the Spartans and the Jews that these two peoples are brothers and that they are of the race of Abraham.
12.22: Now that we know this, you would do well to write to us about your prosperity.
12.23: We too will write to you. Your flocks and your possessions are ours, and ours are yours. The bearers of this letter are instructed to make declarations to this effect.»
12.24: Having been informed that Demetrius' generals had returned to attack him with a larger army than before,
12.25: Jonathan left Jerusalem and marched to meet them as far as the land of Emath, for he did not give them time to invade his land.
12.26: He sent spies into their camp and, upon their return, they reported to him that the Syrians had resolved to surprise him during the night.
12.27: When the sun had set, Jonathan ordered his men to keep watch and remain armed all night, ready to fight, and he detached advanced sentries all around the camp.
12.28: But the enemies, having learned that Jonathan and his people were ready to fight, were seized with fear, they trembled in their hearts, they lit fires in their camp and fled.
12.29: Jonathan and his family did not realize they had retreated until morning, because they saw fires being lit.
12.30: So Jonathan set out in pursuit of them, but he did not catch them, for they had crossed the Eleuthera River.
12.31: Then Jonathan turned to the Arabs called Zabadians, he defeated them and seized their spoils.
12.32: From there he went to Damascus and traveled throughout the whole region.
12.33: Simon, for his part, having set out, advanced as far as Ascalon and the neighboring fortresses, then he turned towards Joppa and occupied it,
12.34: because he had learned that the population intended to hand over the fortress to Demetrius and he placed a garrison there to guard the city.
12.35: Upon his return to Jerusalem, Jonathan summoned the elders of the people and resolved with them to build fortresses in Judea.,
12.36: to raise the walls of Jerusalem and to build a high wall between the citadel and the city, in order to separate the one from the other, so that the citadel would be isolated and neither buying nor selling could take place there.
12.37: With workers gathered to build the city, they went to the wall that rose above the Kidron Valley, towards the east, and repaired the part called Caphenatha.
12.38: Simon, for his part, built Hadida in the Sephelah and put gates and bolts in it.
12.39: However, Tryphon aspired to become king of Asia, to wear the diadem and to seize King Antiochus.
12.40: Fearing that Jonathan would not allow it and would fight against him, he sought a way to seize him and put him to death. So he set out and came to Bethsan.
12.41: Jonathan advanced to meet him, with forty thousand men, elite warriors, and he marched on Bethsan.
12.42: Seeing that Jonathan had come with a large army, Tryphon was afraid to get his hands on him.
12.43: He received him with honor, recommended him to all his friends, offered him gifts, and ordered his troops to obey him as they would themselves.
12.44: And he said to Jonathan, «Why have you wearied all these people, since there is no war between us?
12.45: Send them back to their homes, but choose some of them to accompany you and come with me to Ptolemais. I will deliver that city to you, along with the other fortresses, the other troops, and all the royal officers. Then I will return to Antioch, for that is why I came.»
12.46: Jonathan believed him and did as he had said; he sent his army back, and they returned to Judea.
12.47: He kept three thousand men with him, of whom he detached two thousand in Galilee and only a thousand accompanied him.
12.48: But as soon as Jonathan entered Ptolemais, the inhabitants closed the city gates, seized him, and killed with the sword all those who had entered with him.
12.49: At the same time Tryphon sent an army and cavalry into Galilee and the great plain, to massacre all of Jonathan's men.
12.50: But when they heard that Jonathan had been captured and put to death along with all those who accompanied him, they encouraged one another and set out, their ranks close together, ready to fight.
12.51: Those who were pursuing them, seeing that they were determined to defend their lives, retraced their steps,
12.52: And they all returned to the land of Judah without being disturbed. They wept for Jonathan and his companions, and great fear fell upon them, and all Israel mourned greatly.
12.53: Then all the surrounding nations sought to destroy them, for they said:
12.54: «"They have neither leader nor anyone to help them, so let us attack them now and erase their memory from among men."»

13.1: Simon learned that Tryphon was assembling a considerable army to invade the land of Judah and devastate it.
13.2: Seeing that the people were in fear and terror, he went up to Jerusalem and summoned the people.
13.3: He exhorted them, saying, "You know all that my brothers and I and all my father's house have done to defend our laws and our religion, the battles we have fought and the suffering we have endured.
13.4: That is why all my brothers died for Israel and I was left alone.
13.5: And now, God forbid that I should spare my life in any time of tribulation, for I am no better than my brothers.
13.6: But I want to be the avenger of my people, of the sanctuary, of our wives and our children, for all the nations have united to destroy us out of hatred.»
13.7: The spirit of the people was inflamed upon hearing these words,
13.8: They responded with cheers: "You are our leader, in place of Judas and Jonathan, your brother.
13.9: Lead us into battle and we will do everything you tell us.»
13.10: Then Simon gathered all the fighting men, hastened the completion of the walls of Jerusalem, and fortified the city all around.
13.11: At the same time, he sent Jonathan son of Absalom to Joppa with considerable forces, who, having expelled the inhabitants, remained in that city.
13.12: Tryphon left Ptolemais with a large army to invade the land of Judah, taking Jonathan with him in chains.
13.13: Simon set up his camp at Hadida, opposite the plain.
13.14: When Tryphon learned that Simon had taken command in place of his brother Jonathan and was preparing to fight him, he sent messengers to tell him:
13.15: «"It is because of the money that your brother Jonathan owes to the royal treasury, by reason of the functions he performed, that we are holding him prisoner.
13.100: talents of silver and two of his sons as hostages, so that once he is free, he will not turn against us and we will set him free."»
13.17: Simon understood that the messengers were speaking to him in this way to deceive him, nevertheless he sent the money and the two young children, so as not to attract upon himself great hatred from the people of Israel, who might have said:
13.18: «"It is because Simon did not send the money and the children that Jonathan perished."»
13.19: So he sent the children and the hundred talents of silver, but Tryphon did not keep his word and he did not release Jonathan.
13.20: Then Tryphon advanced to trample the land and devastate it, making a detour, he took the road to Adora, but Simon and his army clung to him wherever he went.
13.21: Those who were in the citadel of Jerusalem sent messengers to Tryphon, begging him to come quickly through the desert and bring them food.
13.22: Tryphon arranged all his cavalry to arrive that night, but very heavy snow fell and he could not reach Jerusalem because of the snow, he left and went to Gilead.
13.23: When he was near Bascama, he killed Jonathan, and Jonathan was buried in that place.
13.24: From there Tryphon returned to his country.
13.25: Simon sent to collect the remains of his brother Jonathan and he buried them in Modin, the city of his fathers.
13.26: All Israel mourned for him deeply and wept for him for many days.
13.27: On the tomb of his father and brothers, Simon had a mausoleum built, high enough to be seen from afar, made of stones polished on the front and back.
13.28: And he had seven pyramids erected above them, facing each other, for his father, for his mother, and for his four brothers.
13.29: He had ornaments made there, surrounding them with tall columns surmounted by suits of armor, as an eternal memorial, and beside the suits of armor he placed sculpted ships to be seen by all who sail on the sea.
13.30: This is the tomb that Simon had erected at Modin and which still exists to this day.
13.31: Tryphon, also using trickery against the young king Antiochus, killed him.
13.32: He reigned in his place and donned the diadem of the kings of Asia and caused great evils in the country.
13.33: Simon rebuilt the fortresses of Judea, equipping them with high towers, high walls, gates and bolts, and he put provisions of food in them.
13.34: Simon chose some men and sent them to King Demetrius so that he would grant remission to Judea, because all of Tryphon's acts were nothing but brigandage.
13.35: King Demetrius replied to him in accordance with these words and wrote him this letter:
13.36: «"King Demetrius to Simon, high priest and friend of kings, to the elders and to the nation of the Jews: Greetings.
13.37: We have received the gold crown and palm that you sent and we are willing to make complete peace with you and to write to the royal stewards to make you various concessions.
13.38: Everything we have decreed concerning you is firm; the fortresses you have built shall be yours.
13.39: We forgive you all the omissions and all the offenses until this day, as well as the crown which you owed, and if any other tribute was levied in Jerusalem, let it not be levied.
13.40: If any of you are willing to enlist in our bodyguard, let them enlist and let peace reign between us.»
13.41: In the year one hundred and seventy, the yoke of the nations was removed from Israel.
13.42: And the people of Israel began to write on the deeds and contracts: «In the first year of Simon, the high priest, commander and governor of the Jews.»
13.43: In those days, Simon marched on Gaza, which he surrounded with his troops, he built rolling siege towers and had them aimed at the city, thus breaching one of the towers and taking possession of it.
13.44: Those who were in the tower jumped into the city, which caused great excitement there.
13.45: The inhabitants, with their wives and children, climbed onto the walls, their clothes torn, shouting loudly and asking Simon to make peace with them:
13.46: «Do not treat us,» they said, “according to our wickedness, but according to your mercy.”
13.47: Simon yielded to their influence and did not fight them anymore, but he banished the inhabitants of the city, purified the houses where there were idols, and entered singing hymns of praise and thanksgiving.
13.48: After removing all impurity from the city, he appointed men to observe the law, then he fortified it and built himself a dwelling there.
13.49: However, those who were in the citadel of Jerusalem, being unable to leave or go into the country, nor to buy or sell, suffered greatly from the famine and a great number died of hunger.
13.50: They loudly begged Simon to make peace with them, which he granted, but he drove them out and cleansed the citadel of all defilement.
13.51: He entered it on the twenty-third day of the second month of the year one hundred and seventy-one, with songs of praise, palm branches, lyres, cymbals, harps, hymns and songs, because a great enemy of Israel was broken.
13.52: He ordered that this day of rejoicing be celebrated every year,
13.53: He fortified the temple mountain, located next to the citadel, and he and his family remained there.
13.54: Then Simon, seeing that his son John was proving himself a man of courage, gave him command of all the troops, with Gazara as his residence.

14.1: In the year one hundred and seventy-two, King Demetrius assembled his armies and went to Media to recruit auxiliary troops to fight Tryphon.
14.2: Arsaces, king of Persia and Media, having learned that Demetrius had entered his territory, sent one of his generals to capture him alive.
14.3: He set out and, having defeated Demetrius' army, he seized him and brought him to Arsaces, who put him in prison.
14.4: The land of Judah was at peace throughout the days of Simon. He strove to bring prosperity to his people, and his authority and glory pleased the people throughout those days.
14.5: Not to mention his other achievements, he took Joppa and made it a port which connected him with the islands of the sea.
14.6: He expanded the borders of his nation and became master of the country.
14.7: He gathered a large number of prisoners, he seized Gazara, Bethsur and the citadel, from which he removed all the filth, and there was no one who could resist him.
14.8: Each person cultivated their land in peace; the soil yielded its produce and the trees in the fields their fruit.
14.9: The old men, sitting in the public squares, all talked about the prosperity of the country, and the young men donned war clothes as an ornament.
14.10: Simon distributed supplies to the cities and provided them with everything necessary for defense: to the point that his glorious name was famous to the ends of the earth.
14.11: He restored peace to his country and Israel rejoiced with great joy.
14.12: Each one sat under his own vine and fig tree, and no one inspired fear in them.
14.13: There was no longer any adversary to attack them in the country; the enemy kings were defeated in those days.
14.14: He was the support of all the unfortunate of his people, he showed zeal for the law and eliminated all the impious and wicked.
14.15: He glorified the sanctuary and multiplied the sacred utensils.
14.16: When news of Jonathan's death reached Rome and even Sparta, they were deeply grieved.
14.17: But when they learned that Simon, his brother, was high priest in his place and ruler of the whole land and all its cities,
14.18: They wrote to him on bronze tablets to renew with him the alliance and friendship they had made with Judas and Jonathan, his brothers.
14.19: The letters were read in the presence of the entire assembly in Jerusalem, and here is a copy of the one the Spartans sent:
14.20: «"The leaders of the Spartans and the city to Simon the high priest, to the elders, the priests, and the rest of the Jewish people, their brothers: Greetings.
14.21: The ambassadors who were sent to our people told us of the glory and honor you enjoy, and we rejoiced at their arrival.
14.22: And we have recorded among the decisions of the people what was said by them, namely: Numenius, son of Antiochus and Antipater, son of Jason, ambassadors of the Jews, came to us to renew friendship with us.
14.23: And it pleased the people to receive these men with honor and to deposit a copy of their speeches in the public archives, so that the people of Sparta might preserve the memory of them, and we had this copy written down for Simon the high priest.»
14.24: After that, Simon sent Numenius to Rome, with a large gold shield weighing a thousand minae, to secure the alliance with them.
14.25: When the people heard these things, they said, «What thanks shall we give to Simon and his sons?
14.26: For he showed unwavering steadfastness; he, his brothers, and his father's house fought and drove out the enemies of Israel and secured their freedom.» They engraved these things on bronze tablets, which they hung on a pillar on Mount Zion.
14.27: Here is the copy: «On the eighteenth day of the month of Elul, in the year one hundred and seventy-two, in the third year of Simon the high priest, in Saramel,
14.28: In the great assembly of priests and people, of the princes of the nation and the elders of the land, the following was proclaimed: «In the many battles which have taken place in our country,
14.29: Simon son of Mattathias, of the descendants of Jarib and his brothers, exposed themselves to danger and resisted the enemies of their nation, so that their sanctuary and the law might remain standing, and they obtained great glory for their nation.
14.30: Jonathan gathered his nation and became their high priest, then he was reunited with his people.
14.31: Their enemies wanted to trample their country and devastate it and lay hands on their sanctuary.
14.32: So Simon arose and fought for his nation; he spent much of his own wealth, provided weapons to the valiant men of his nation, and gave them a salary.,
14.33: He fortified the cities of Judea, as well as Bethsuri, located on the border, where the weapons of the enemies had previously been kept, and he placed a garrison of Jewish troops there.
14.34: He fortified Joppa, located on the sea, and Gazara on the border of Azotus, formerly inhabited by the enemies, and he established Jews there and supplied them with everything necessary for their recovery.
14.35: The people saw Simon's conduct and the glory he intended to give to his nation, and they made him their leader and high priest because of all the services he had rendered them, and because of the justice and faithfulness he maintained toward his nation, and because he worked in every way to exalt his people.
14.36: «While he lived, everything prospered in his hands, to the point that he drove out the nations from the land they occupied, as well as those who were in the city of David in Jerusalem, who had built themselves a citadel from which they made sorties, defiling the surroundings of the sanctuary and greatly profaning its holiness.
14.37: He settled Jewish warriors there and fortified it to ensure the defense of the country and the city, and he raised the walls of Jerusalem.
14.38: King Demetrius consequently granted him the supreme priesthood,
14.39: He declared him his friend and bestowed upon him the highest honors.
14.40: For he had learned that the Romans called the Jews friends and allies and brothers and that they had honorably received Simon's envoys.
14.41: «"So the Jews and the priests were pleased that Simon should be prince and high priest forever, until a trustworthy prophet should appear,
14.42: that he command their armies, that he take care of sacred things, that he appoint officers for public services, to administer the country, to oversee armaments and defend fortresses,
14.43: that he take care of holy things, that he be obeyed by all, that all public acts in the country be written in his name, and that he be clothed in purple and gold.
14.44: No one from the people or from among the priests shall be permitted to reject any of these points, to contradict any order given by him, to convene any assembly in the land without his permission, to wear a purple robe or a gold clasp.
14.45: Anyone who acts contrary to this decree or violates any article thereof will incur punishment.
14.46: «"It seemed good to the people to invest Simon with the power to act according to this decree.
14.47: Simon accepted; he was willing to fulfill the functions of high priest, commander of the armies, and governor of the Jews and priests, and to exercise supreme command.»
14.48: It was decided to engrave this document on bronze tablets and place them prominently in the temple gallery.,
14.49: and to place a copy of it in the treasury, for use by Simon and his sons.

15.1: King Antiochus, son of Demetrius, sent a letter from the islands of the sea to Simon, high priest and governor of the Jews, and to the entire nation,
15.2: It was worded as follows: "King Antiochus, to Simon, high priest and governor, and to the nation of the Jews: greetings.
15.3: Since wretches have seized the kingdom of our fathers, and I wish to reclaim it in order to restore it to its former state, and since I have gathered numerous troops and equipped many warships,
15.4: intending to land in the country to take revenge on those who have ruined our country and devastated many cities in this kingdom,
15.5: I confirm to you all the tribute remissions granted to you by my predecessor kings and all the other remissions they have granted you.
15.6: I allow you to mint coins bearing your imprint for your country.
15.7: May Jerusalem and the temple be free, and may all the weapons you have made and the fortresses you have built and occupy remain in your possession.
15.8: May everything owed or due to the royal treasury be given to you now and forever.
15.9: When we have regained possession of our kingdom, we will magnificently honor you, your nation, and the sanctuary, so that your glory will shine throughout the universe.»
15.10: In the year one hundred and seventy-four, Antiochus set out for the land of his fathers and all the troops came to rally around him, so that few men remained at Tryphon.
15.11: King Antiochus went in pursuit of him and Tryphon came fleeing to Dora, on the sea.
15.12: For he saw that evils were piling up upon him and that his army was abandoning him.
15.13: Antiochus came to camp in front of Dora with one hundred and twenty thousand combatants and eight thousand horsemen.
15.14: He besieged the city and, as ships approached from the sea, he pressed upon it both by land and by sea, letting no one enter or leave.
15.15: However, Numenius and those who had accompanied him arrived from the city of Rome with letters addressed to kings and countries; here is their content:
15.16: «Lucius, Roman consul to King Ptolemy: Greetings.
15.17: The ambassadors of the Jews came to us as our friends and allies, to renew the ancient friendship and alliance, having been sent by the high priest Simon and by the Jewish people.
15.18: They brought a shield of gold worth a thousand mines.
15.19: That is why it seemed good to us to write to kings and countries not to cause them harm, not to attack them, their cities, or their country, and not to lend assistance to those who would wage war against them.
15.20: It seemed good to us to receive the shield from them.
15.21: »If any wicked men have fled from their own country to yours, hand them over to Simon the high priest so that he may punish them according to their own law.”
15.22: The same letter was addressed to King Demetrius, Attalus, Ariarathes, and Arsaces,
15.23: as well as to all countries: to Lampsacus, to the Spartans, to Delos, to Myndus, to Sicyon, to Caria, to Samos, to Pamphylia, to Lycia, to Halicarnassus, to Rhodes, to Phaselis, to Kos, to Side, to Aradus, to Gortyn, to Cnidus, to Cyprus and to Cyrene.
15.24: They made a copy of this letter for Simon, the high priest.
15.25: King Antiochus attacked Dora on the second day, bringing his troops ever closer and building machines, and he imprisoned Tryphon so that no one could enter or leave.
15.26: So Simon sent him a relief force of two thousand elite men, as well as money, gold, and considerable supplies.
15.27: The king refused to receive them, but he revoked all previous commitments he had made to Simon and treated him as a stranger.
15.28: He sent Athenobius, one of his friends, to communicate with him and tell him: "You occupy Joppa, Gazara and the citadel of Jerusalem, which are cities of my kingdom.
15.29: You have devastated their surroundings, causing great havoc in the country, and you have taken control of many places that are part of my states.
15.30: Now therefore, hand over to us the cities which you have seized and the tributes of the localities which you have taken possession of, outside the territory of Judea.
15.31: Otherwise, give us five hundred talents of silver instead, and another five hundred talents for the devastation you have caused and for the tribute owed by these cities; failing that, we will go to war against you.»
15.32: Athenobius, a friend of the king, having arrived in Jerusalem, saw the magnificence of Simon, a sideboard covered with gold and silver vases and the great pomp with which he was surrounded, he was astonished and he repeated the words of the king.
15.33: Simon answered him, «It is not foreign land that we have taken, nor have we seized the possessions of others, but the inheritance of our fathers, which for a time was unjustly possessed by our enemies.
15.34: For us, finding the opportunity favorable, we claim the heritage of our fathers.
15.35: As for Joppa and Gazara, which you claim, these two cities did much harm to our people and in our own land; we will give one hundred talents for them.» Athenobius did not answer him a word.,
15.36: But he returned angrily to the king and reported Simon's reply, the magnificence of his court, and all that he had seen, which threw the king into great anger.
15.37: However, Tryphon fled to Orthosias on a ship.
15.38: The king appointed Cendébée commander of the coast and gave him an army of infantry and cavalry.
15.39: And he ordered him to establish his camp opposite Judea, to fortify Gedor, to secure its gates, and to wage war against the people. The king, however, pursued Tryphon.
15.40: Cendébée, having gone to Jamnia, began to anger the people, to invade Judea, to take prisoners and to massacre. He fortified Gédor
15.41: and he placed cavalry and foot troops there, to make sorties and infest the roads of Judea, as the king had commanded him.

16.1: John came up from Gazara and told his father what Cendébée was doing.
16.2: Simon called his two eldest sons, Judah and John, and said to them, «My brothers and I and my father’s house have fought against the enemies of Israel from our youth until this day, and we have often succeeded by our own hands in saving Israel.
16.3: Now I am old, and you, by the grace of God, are old enough; take my place and that of my brother, go and fight for our nation, and may heaven's help be with you.»
16.4: Then he chose twenty thousand fighters and horsemen from the country, who marched against Cendébée, they camped the night at Modin.
16.5: Having risen in the morning, they advanced towards the plain and behold, a large army of infantry and cavalry came to meet them, separated by the bed of a torrent.
16.6: John and his men set up camp opposite them. Noticing that his troops were trembling to cross the torrent, he crossed it first; having seen this, his warriors crossed after him.
16.7: He divided his army into two corps, placing the cavalry between the infantry, but the enemy cavalry was very numerous.
16.8: They sounded the trumpets and Cendébée was put to flight with his army, many fell struck down and the rest sought refuge in the fortress.
16.9: Then Judas, John's brother, was wounded, but John pursued the fugitives until he came to Gedor, which Cendébée had fortified.
16.10: The vanquished fled to the towers in the fields of Azotus, and he set the city on fire. Two thousand of them perished, and John returned in peace to Judea.
16.11: Ptolemy, son of Abobus, had been appointed military governor of the plain of Jericho; he possessed a great deal of gold and silver.,
16.12: because he was the son-in-law of the high priest.
16.13: His heart became proud, he aspired to become master of the land, and he plotted treacherous schemes against Simon and his sons, to destroy them.
16.14: Now Simon, who was inspecting the cities of Judah, concerned himself with their welfare, went down to Jericho, he, Mattathias his son and Judah, in the year one hundred and seventy-seven, in the eleventh month, which is the month of Sabbath.
16.15: Abobus' son received them by trickery in a small fortress, named Doch, which he had built, he prepared a great feast for them and kept men hidden there.
16.16: When Simon and his sons became drunk, Ptolemy got up with his men and, seizing their weapons, they rushed upon Simon in the banquet hall and massacred him along with his two sons and some servants.
16.17: He thus committed a great betrayal and returned evil for good.
16.18: Ptolemy immediately wrote to the king to inform him of the event and to ask him to send troops to his aid, so that he would hand over the country and the cities of the Jews.
16.19: He dispatched other emissaries to Gazara to kill John and sent letters to the generals, summoning them to him, to give them money, gold and gifts.
16.20: He sent still others to occupy Jerusalem and the temple mountain.
16.21: But a messenger, having gone ahead, came to announce to John, in Gazara, the murder of his father and brothers, and he added: "He has also sent assassins to kill you.".
16.22: At this news, John was greatly disturbed; he seized the men who came to kill him and had them killed, for he recognized that they intended to kill him.
16.23: The rest of John's story, his wars, the exploits he accomplished, the walls he had built, and all his actions,
16.24: This is all written in the Annals of his high priesthood, from the day he became high priest after his father.
